dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond for unconscious incident at upstairs unit of adult care home in Richmond.
Hello, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What prompted the 911 call?
A 57-year-old man was found unconscious upstairs, so we called.
Let's check his responsiveness. Presently he has a low Glasgow Coma Scale.
Eyes open minimally to physical stimulation, but no verbal response.
No verbal response - appears to be in altered mental status.
No motor response to pain or verbal cues.
Airway appears patent, with snoring respirations observed.
Breathing is 14 per minute - no signs of labored respiration noted.
Equal bilateral breath sounds - nothing remarkable is present.
Strong pulses palpable, no signs of circulation compromise detected.
Blood pressure is 167/95 - skin is warm and dry.
What is his exact age and do you see any injuries?
He's 57, and we don't see any injuries or wounds.
Any symptoms before unresponsiveness or prior distress?
No prior response - just found unresponsive, in apparent hypoglycemic crisis.
What are his known conditions or medication history?
Diabetes-insulin dependent, hypertension, asthma, schizoaffective disorder.
Which medications is he currently on?
Novolin N, Humulin N, Haloperidol, Lisinopril, Tricor, Aspirin, Symbicort - no known allergies.
When was the last time he ate or drank according to the chart or staff notes?
No specific time noted-staff was unsure if he skipped a meal or took too much insulin.
Has he had similar episodes or events recently, maybe fainting or near-fainting?
Yes, he experienced similar hypoglycemic episodes. Recently admitted for leg pain, possibly from old injury.
Any possible cause for this episode mentioned in the chart or statement on file?
Not specific, but staff suspects diabetes-related disorientation and AMS must be considered.
Any signs of pain or discomfort previously recorded or signs now? Rate pain from 0 to 10 if possible.
Patient remains pain-free; historical record indicates no complaints - score is zero currently.
Pulse is 71 - regular, no changes from initial assessment so far.
Respiratory rate is 14 - stable and no signs of respiratory distress detected.
Blood pressure is currently 167/95 - outlining the need for glucose administration first.
Blood glucose reading is 36 mg/dL - that is critically low, not previously verified prior to EMS.
Oxygen saturation is 99% - stable at point of initial evaluation.
EKG traces indicate sinus rhythm.
Preparing to administer dextrose IV bolus as per hypoglycemia protocol.
Obtaining 18G IV access for bolus of dextrose to address the hypoglycemic episode.
Patient is now alert and oriented x4, Glasgow Coma Scale improved from 3 to 15.
Rechecking pulse - remains steady at 73, no immediate concerns again noted.
Respiration is 17 per minute - still no signs of distress or shortness of breath detected yet.
Blood pressure has slightly declined - now 146/86, which falls within acceptable range for condition.
Oxygen saturation at 95% - that is acceptable and improving with treatment so far.
Skin continues to appear warm, dry, and clear of rashes, sores, or petechiae observed in patient.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive - no signs of facial droop observed.
Normal bilateral breath sounds - patient denies chest discomfort.
Assessing no signs of injury or cardiac compromise - ECG shows non-ischemic rhythm.
Applying continuous 4-lead EKG monitoring per standing protocol instructions issued.
Motor and sensory functions appear intact in all limbs - no signs of DCAP-BTLS observed.
Found no visible injuries, abrasions, or signs of trauma upon second routine body scan of patient.
20:46 - pulse remains steady - 72 - which is considered stable and sustained so far in progress.
Respiration rate at 15 - no changes - patient tolerating everything better than at initial scene.
Current blood pressure reading is 128/80 - within reasonable window with rising glucose levels detected.
Latest glucose reading is now 218 mg/dL - correction is clear and occurs rapidly post-dextrose.
Oxygen saturation has improved - now at 98% - much better from prior baseline at scene.
Rechecking - EKG remains normal sinus rhythm, no arrhythmias or other ECG-abnormalities present.
Patient secured onto a Reeve's stretcher - Fire assisted with safe transport downstairs to transport vehicle.
Fire team carried patient into ambulance with no problems - stretcher secured to transport vehicle with no notable casualty movement during descent in the residential home.
Harness is secured properly - per standard immobilization protocol for this condition.
Patient is being transported to St. Mary's, according to staff on site request.
Transport is proceeding smoothly - no changes in status or vitals noted.
Communication with ED has been established en route - patient status and EMS plan updated.
Patient was wheeled directly to room 10 upon arrival - all records and interventions transferred.
Handover was smooth and team closely coordinated - all field notes and interventions were shared accurately.
Nuchal rigidity not noted and no signs of lymphadenopathy observed on initial or reassessment.
No hemorrhage or drainage noted in ears, nose, or throat. No sunken eyes observed.
Per prior assessments and reassessment, heart sounds are normal with no murmurs or abnormalities.
No masses, rigid abdomen, or abnormal sounds noted during evaluation.
No rectal bleeding or signs of genitourinary compromise.
No deformity, angulation, or abnormal use of extremities noted on examination or reassessments.
Vertebral column, flank, and buttocks are stable - no signs of trauma.
Repeating glucose reading at 20:46 confirms marked improvement to 218 mg/dL.
Continuous EKG confirmed no IS, BBB, or ST changes - presents with stable sinus rhythm.
Performed one attempted procedure successfully for IV access and administered bolus as per order.
Confirmation of safe transport destination received via facility requesting transport and ED en route.
All restraints verified - patient transported in secure position as per protocol.
Patient placed on stretcher with assistance from hospital staff, as per decontamination and transfer guidelines.
Receiving facility signature on EMS form has been observed - patient handed over officially.
No new pain complaints noted - score remains zero.
No change in signs and symptoms - patient remains asymptomatic of pain or distress.
No defined time still - staff remains unsure about meal timing.
Similar event in history due to old injury and possible insulin administration.
No further interventions required - patient stable, responsive.
Reassessed skin - remains unremarkable - still warm, dry, and no rashes present.
Per routine check, no need to repeat - glucose levels stable, improving.
No ST segment changes, arrhythmias, or other deficits on latest read - normal rhythm confirmed.
Reevaluated stretcher placement and harness - noted as secured continually for safe transport.
No alternative sites required - destination appropriate for condition as judged by staff.
Debrief shared with ED staff including dextrose administration, IV placement, and prior hospital visit compliance.
Final documentation completed - receiving facility completed sign-off for EMS template.
All secondary assessments reviewed after interventions - all systems stable - no findings of concern.
No changes or interruptions in rhythm - condition stable for transport.
No contraindications for transfer noted - transport system fully compliant and locked in place.
ECW monitoring has ceased, as patient is stable, per hospital arrival process.
Rechecked airway, breathing, and circulation - all stable, no new compromise present.
Airway patent and no obstruction noted after intervention.
Breathing normal per reassess - no signs of distress at latest.
Peripheral pulses present and equal - skin non-dry but warm and normal prior to removal and handoff.
No changes in site selection or patient status.
Handover details, including medications and IV, were passed to receiving nurse as required.
Patient movement secured with full transfer to hospital bed via sheet draw with no incident.
Final destination confirmed - transported to correct facility.
